biography text: Abboud studied art at the Academy of Fine Arts, Beirut in 1947. He then went to Paris where he trained in the ateliers of Cubists Fernand Léger and André Lhote. In 1962 Abboud recommenced his studies at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts. Throughout his career, Abboud received different international and regional awards such as the Victor Choquet Prize in France in 1961 and the Sursock Museum Prize in Lebanon in 1964. He participated in the first Paris Biennale in 1959.

Abdalla studied at the School of Applied Arts and the School of Decorative Arts in Cairo from 1931 to 1935, focusing on wrought-iron design. He debuted at the Salon du Caire in 1938, and later founded his own private art school in 1943, where he taught and advised many prominent Egyptian artists, including Inji Efflatoun and his first wife and protégée Taheya Halim. He traveled in Europe extensively after World War II, and eventually relocated to Copenhagen in 1956. Ten years later he moved to Paris, where he spent the remainder of his life.

Ben Abdallah studied in Tunis at the Lycée Carnot before moving away from the city in 1939 to the northern town of Sidi Bou Said. He was considered a contributing artist of the École de Tunis, a school of painting which flourished between the 1940s and 1970s.

An artist, writer and poet, Adnan received a degree in philosophy from the Sorbonne in Paris in the 1950s before moving to the United States, where she continued her graduate studies at the University of California in Berkeley, and at Harvard University. Adnan has published many books in English, Arabic, and French, including poetry, essays, and the novel Sitt Marie Rose in 1978. For many years she taught philosophy of art at the Dominican University of California in San Rafael, and has lectured at universities throughout the United States.

Ahmed completed a degree in Painting at the Faculty of Fine Arts in Cairo in 1959. He worked at the Cairo train Station and for National Egyptian Television. In 1963 he received a grant to work as an artist full-time for seven consecutive years. Ahmad participated in several biennials, including Zagreb, Baghdad, Venice, and Paris.

Ahmad earned his Bachelor's Degree in Arts and Education from Helwan University, Cairo, in 1976 before going on to pursue a MFA from Mills College in California. He trained in classical calligraphy, and worked as a calligrapher and a caricaturist. After completing his studies, Ahmad took up a post at Qatar University, where he taught art over twenty years. Ahmad won First Prize at the Baghdad Festival in 1988, and was the director of the Arab Museum of Modern Art in Qatar when the collection was still privately owned.

Ahmed began studying art at the Academy of Fine Arts in Baghdad in 1959, and continued his studies in Moscow, where he earned a Ph.D. in 1979. As both a professor of art and an artist, Ahmed has participated in many international art conferences, and has published on the subjects of Islamic and modern art. He was a member of the Pioneers Group when it was active, and belongs to the Society of Fine Arts in Iraq.

Aleem spent many years in the United States attending both the Pratt Institute in New York and the University of Southern California, where she completed her Master's Degree in Graphic Art in 1957. Upon her return to Cairo, she earned a Ph.D. from Helwan University, and began teaching at Alexandria's Faculty of Fine Arts in 1968. She was made Head of the Design Department, a position she held until her final appointment as Professor Emerita.

Arabi studied painting at the Faculty of Fine Arts at Damascus University before receiving a diploma from the Higher Institute of Fine Arts in Paris in 1983. He then earned a Ph.D. from the Sorbonne in 1987. A French citizen and resident of Paris since 1975, Arabi's art scholarship and writings have been published in both French and Arabic.

Artist, professor and critic, Arif graduated from the College of Fine Arts, Baghdad in 1956, gained his MFA from the Surikov Academy, Moscow in 1967, and Ph.D. in the Philosophy of Art from Salah al-Din University in Arbil (Iraq) in 2004. Arif was one of the founders of Arbil Fine Arts College and also a member of the Baghdad Modern Art Group. In 1974 he became a member of the International Association of Art. In addition, he published books and essays on applied art and art theory in Arabic, Kurdish, and Russian.

Al-Attar graduated from the Academy of Fine Arts, Baghdad, in 1965. She was the director of the Iraqi National Museum, as well as a member of the Iraqi Artists Society. She represented Iraq in various biennials, and won several regional awards including the Golden Sail Medal at the Cairo Biennial in 1984. Al-Attar was killed in 1993 by a US missile in Iraq.

Al-Attar obtained a degree in fine art from the Academy of Fine Arts in Baghdad, and another from California State University. Later she received degrees in printmaking from the Wimbledon School of Art and the Central School of Art and Design in London. She is a member of the Iraqi Artists Society, and has exhibited widely, participating in regional and international biennials. She has received numerous awards such as at the 3rd World Biennial of Graphic Art in London in 1978.

Awad trained at the Académie Libanaise des Beaux-Arts before moving to Paris in 1947 to further his studies at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts. During this time he also worked in the studios of the aging Fauvists Othon Friesz and André Lhote. Although he attempted to settle back in Beirut in 1951, Awad eventually returned to live in Paris.

Ayad was a student at the School of Fine Arts in Cairo when it first opened in 1908, and in 1925 he was the first to be awarded a government scholarship to study in Rome at the Accademia di Belle Arti. Five years later he returned to Cairo and was appointed director of the College of Applied Arts. He then taught at the School of Fine Arts in Cairo, and later was made director of the Museum of Modern Art in Cairo in 1950.

Azzawi graduated with a degree in Archaeology from the University of Baghdad in 1962 followed by a degree in Fine Arts from the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad in 1964. He founded the art group New Vision in 1969 with Mohammed Muhriddin, Ismail Fattah, Hashem al-Samarchi and Saleh al-Jumaie, and was later also a member of the One Dimension group established by Shakir Hassan Al Said in 1971. He worked with the Department of Antiquities in Baghdad until 1976, when he moved to London to work with the Iraqi Cultural Center.

Bachi completed his studies in Arabic Literature at Damascus University in 1957, and then pursued painting at the Academy of Arts in Berlin from 1957 to 1962. He was a professor of oil painting at the Faculty of Fine Arts in Berlin from 1977 to 2002. Bachi has been a member of the Akademie der Künste since 1993, and now teaches at the Hochschule der Künste in Berlin.

Banani graduated from the Royal School of Fine Arts in Cairo in 1937 with a degree in art education. Three years later, in 1940 el-Banani completed his Master's degree from the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome. He then returned to Cairo, joining the College of Fine Arts as a teacher in 1951, eventually becoming the head of the Painting department.

An art historian and artist, Boullata graduated from the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome in 1965 and studied at the Corcoran Museum Art School in Washington, D.C. in 1971. Boullata was the recipient in 1993 of a Fulbright Senior Scholar Fellowship to research Islamic art in Morocco. He has published journal articles and essays on contemporary and modern Arab art in both English and Arabic, as well as several books, including Palestinian Art: From 1850 to the Present in 2009.

Cherkaoui studied graphics at the École des Métiers d'Art from 1956 to 1959, and then studied at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in Paris for a year, before moving to Warsaw in 1961 to continue his studies in fine art. Cherkaoui returned to Paris, where he won a research grant to study Moroccan signs and symbols and participated in the Salon de Mai. He traveled frequently between Europe and North Africa.

Choucair began her studies at the American University in Beirut in philosophy. She informally studied under two leading Lebanese artists, Omar Onsi and Mustafa Farrouk. From 1948 to 1952 she continued her studies at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in Paris. Choucair won the grand prize at the Alexandria Biennial in 1968, and in 1970 began exhibiting in the Salon de Mai in Paris. She later went on to teach at the Lebanese University beginning in 1977, and lectured at the American University in Beirut in 1986.

In 1870 Corm went to Rome to train under the Italian court painter Roberto Bompiani at the Accademia di San Luca. Pope Plus IX commissioned a portrait from him, and soon was appointed the court painter for King Leopold II of Belgium. Corm returned to Lebanon in 1878 and traveled widely within the Arab region, painting portraits of dignitaries. Corm received many national awards during his lifetime, including the Cavalier Medal of Saint Gregory, the Ottoman Medal of Glory First Degree, and the Lebanese Order of Merit.

Georges Corm, son of the renowned painter Daoud Corm, studied at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in Paris from 1919 to 1921, and won the Gold Medal at the Beirut Exhibition Fair in 1921. In 1928 he moved to Egypt, where he worked on portrait commissions and taught painting. By 1957 Corm returned to his native Beirut and received the Lebanese Order of the Cedar in 1958.

Al-Dabbagh obtained a degree in painting from the Institute of Fine Art in Baghdad in 1965. From 1967 to 1968 he studied graphic design in Lisbon, Portugal. He presently teaches at the Institute of Fine Art in Baghdad.

country of residence: Tunis and Sidi Bou Said, Tunisia
biography text: Dahak studied at the École des Beaux Arts de Tunis in 1950 and in Rome at the Accademia di Belle Arti in 1961 under the artist Amerigo Bartoli. Dahak established the Foundation for Graphic Arts in Tunis, and generated wider acceptance for graphic art as a form in Tunisia. Dahak was considered a member of the École de Tunis.

As a young man Douaihy apprenticed with Lebanese painter Habib Srour for four years, and then graduated in 1932 from the École Nationale Supérieure des Arts Décoratifs in Paris. He returned home and established his own studio in Beirut, and intermittently painted frescoes and designed stained glass for the Church of Diman, and later other churches in Lebanon. In 1950, Douaihy immigrated to New York. He was awarded the Lebanese National Order of the Cedar in 1968 and the Medaglia d'Oro of the Accademia d'Italia delle Arti e del Lavoro in 1980.

Al-Droubi began his art education at the Accademia Reale in Rome, and later went to London to study at Goldsmiths College in 1950. He established the first freelance atelier in Baghdad in 1942, and founded the experimental Impressionists Group in 1953. Al-Droubi was the head of the Society of Iraqi Plastic Arts for six years, and the dean of the Academy of Fine Arts in Baghdad. He played a vital role in setting up an academic foundation for art education in Iraq.

In 1958 Durra graduated from the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome. After a brief stint teaching at the Teachers Training College in Amman, he spent the next decade in residence in Rome. In 1970 he returned to Jordan and received the National Award. Durra has been credited with establishing an academic art appreciation in Jordan.

Al-Futaih is one of the first artists in Yemen to have established a professional career. He studied in Germany for a degree in fine art at the Kunstakademie in Düsseldorf, followed by a Ph.D. in Art History from the University of Cologne. Since his return to Yemen, Al-Futaih has played an important role in developing his country's art scene. He opened the first exhibition hall in Yemen, and established the National Art Center.

After moving to Cairo with his family as a young boy, el-Gazzar joined the Artistic Club, led by the renowned artist Hussein Youssef Amin at the Helmiyya Secondary School. By 1950 he had completed a degree in painting at the School of Fine Arts in Cairo, where he later became an assistant professor of photography. El-Gazzar was an essential constituent of the Contemporary Art Group founded by Hussein Youssef Amin in 1946. He won several awards throughout his career, including a bronze medal from an exhibition in São Paulo, and a gold medal from the Arab Salon in Rome, both in 1957.

In 1927, Gemayel abandoned his interests in pharmacology to pursue his interests in painting at the Académie Julian, Paris. Encouraged by his mentor Khalil Saleeby, Gemayel won the First Prize at the 1931 Paris Exhibition Coloniale Internationale. He returned to Beirut and continued to draw and paint, and in 1937, with the founding of Académie Libanaise des Beaux-Arts, he started to teach.

As a young adult, Gharbaoui took night classes in painting at the Academy of Arts in Fez. He continued his studies at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts and the Académie Julian in Paris from 1952 until 1958, when the Italian government awarded him a scholarship and he moved to Rome. In 1960 he returned to Morocco and established a studio in Chellah, on the outskirts of Rabat.

Of Armenian descent, Guiragossian studied art at the Yarkon Studio in Jerusalem before immigrating with his family to Lebanon in 1948. He won a scholarship to the Accademia di Belle Arti in Florence in 1957. He won First Prize at both the 1958 Florence Biennale and the 1961 Paris Biennale. He was made Knight of the Order of Arts and Letters by the French Ministry of Culture in 1984, and was posthumously awarded the Lebanese Medal of Honor of the Cedar.

Halim grew up in Egypt, where her father was King Fuad's chamberlain. She trained with several well established artists in Egypt at the time, including Hamed Abdalla, whom she married in 1943. She studied at the Académie Julian in Paris, and returned to Cairo, where she taught painting.

Due to the 1948 Nakba, al-Hallaj's family relocated to Damascus when he was ten. Al-Hallaj trained as a sculptor at the Fine Arts Academy in Cairo, and completed his postgraduate studies at the Luxor Atelier in 1964. A fierce advocate for Palestine, al-Hallaj was a founding member of the General Union of Palestinian Writers and Journalists, and a member of the General Union of Palestinian Abstract Artists in Syria. Al-Hallaj died tragically while rescuing his work from a fire in his studio.

Hammad studied at the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome from 1953 to 1957. Upon his return to his homeland, his fascination with calligraphy evolved into an abstract approach toward letters, words, and forms. He held a position as art professor at the Faculty of Fine Arts in Damascus, and was appointed the Dean of the Faculty of Fine Arts in Damascus until his retirement in 1980. He contributed to the creation of the Unknown Soldier Monument in Damascus, erected after his death in 1994.
A 1946 graduate of the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad, Hammoudi earned a government scholarship to study in Paris.

Helmy received a diploma from the High Institute for Artistic Education in 1949. In 1952 she was awarded a governmental scholarship to study at the Slade School of Art in London, where she completed her degree in 1955. Later she was given the position of Professor Emerita of Graphics at the Accademia di Belle Arti, Florence. Helmy worked as an instructor at several Cairo institutions, including the Fine Arts Institute in Cairo, the Artistic Education Institution, and Helwan University.

Hemche began his artistic education at the École des Beaux-Arts in Algiers. He taught art in 1927 in Fez and followed that by working as an inspector of Moroccan art in 1929. Hemche won the Jean Bevier Award and received a grant from the city of Algiers. He moved to Paris to study at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in 1938. Years later, after 1962, he took up a position as a teacher there.

Born into a family of metalworkers, Henein received a degree in Sculpture from the School of Fine Arts in Cairo in 1953. He later studied at the Akademie der Bildenden Künste in Munich. Soon after completion of his training, Henein received a two-year grant from the Egyptian government. In 1971 he moved to Paris, where he focused primarily on producing abstract paintings on papyrus. In 1990 Henein was offered a leading role in restoring Giza's iconic Sphinx, a first step in moving back to Egypt, which was finalized once he committed to establishing a yearly International Sculpture Symposium in Aswan, Egypt, in 1996, which he directs to this day.

Hikmet graduated from the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad in 1953. A protégé of Jewad Selim, he was an influential member of the Baghdad Modern Art Group. He went on to graduate from the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome in 1959. When he returned to Iraq, having studied bronze casting at the Institute of Zaka in 1961, Hikmet assisted Salim with his Monument for Freedom, and supervised its completion after Salim's death. He also taught sculpture at his alma mater and at the College of Engineering at Baghdad University, and created public sculptures in cities throughout Iraq.
Al-Jaafari studied oil painting at the Faculty of Fine Arts in Cairo from 1944 to 1947. He returned to Syria and taught art at secondary schools, and later the Faculty of Fine Arts in Damascus.

Al-Jader graduated from the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad, 1946. He moved to Paris and earned his Ph.D. in the History of Islamic Art at the Sorbonne, a degree from the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in 1954, and joined the Salon de Paris. Upon his return to Iraq he took a position as dean of the Institute of Fine Arts for several years, and became a member of the Pioneers Group, the Impressionists Group, and was the head of the Society of Iraqi Artists.

In 1960 al-Kaabi completed his studies in fine arts in Baghdad. In the succeeding decades he was integral to the Iraqi Modernist movement with such contemporaries as Jamil Hammoudi and Ismail Fattah. From 1981 to 1985 al-Kaabi worked as Director of Theatre Production at the Cinema and Theatre Foundation in Baghdad, and as the Director of the Department of Planning at the Iraqi Ministry of Culture. He later became President of the Iraqi Plastic Artists Association.

Kamel was one of the first graduates of the School of Fine Arts in Cairo in 1911, and went to Rome to continue his studies in 1921. When he returned to Cairo he joined the Society of Fine Arts and taught Painting at the Higher Institute of Fine Arts, where he became Head of the Painting Department in 1937. Kamel was Director of the Museum of Modern Art in Egypt from 1948 to 1949 and Dean of the Faculty of Fine Arts from 1950 until his retirement in 1953. Kamel received the Egyptian State Merit Award in 1960.

Kayyali received a government scholarship to study art and graduated from the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome in 1961. While in Italy he won several awards, and along with Fateh Moudarres, Kayyali represented Syria in the 1960 Venice Biennale. On his return to Syria he taught art at a Damascus state school, and later served as professor of the Interior Design department at the Faculty of Fine Arts at the University of Damascus.

In 1953 el-Nagdi graduated from the Faculty of Fine Arts at Helwan University and in 1957, he completed a degree at the Faculty of Applied Arts in Cairo. In 1959 el-Nagdi studied ceramics while on scholarship in Russia, and received a series of art diplomas in Italy between 1960 and 1964, from the Accademia di Belle Arti in Venice, the Accademia di Belle Arti in Ravenna ,and the Ruskin Artistic Appreciation Institute, also in Venice. He won the Grand Prize at the Alexandria Biennials of 1966 and 1974.

Nagy studied law in Lyon, France, from 1906 to 1910, and was the first Egyptian to study art in Florence, from 1910 to 1914. In 1932 he founded the Alexandria Atelier, and three years later, the Cairo Atelier for Artists and Writers. From 1937 to 1939 he was the first Egyptian Director of the School of Fine Arts in Cairo, also his alma mater. Nagy later became Director of the Museum of Modern Art in Cairo from 1939 until 1947, when he became President of the Egyptian Academy in Rome. In 1962 the Egyptian Ministry of Culture converted his Cairo studio into a museum.

Al-Nasiri studied painting at the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad from 1956 to 1959, printmaking in the Central Academy of Fine Arts in Beijing from 1959 to 1963, and won a scholarship to study art at the Gravura engravers' cooperative in Lisbon, Portugal, from 1967 to 1969. He founded and led the Graphic Arts Department at the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad, and also taught painting, graphic art, and graphic design in Jordan and Bahrain. A winner of international and regional prizes, al-Nasiri has published books and essays on Iraqi art, and a book entitled Contemporary Graphic Art in 1997.

Nawar gained a BFA in Graphic Art from Helwan University in 1967 and went on to obtain a degree in graphics and murals from the Real Academia de Bellas Artes de San Fernando in Madrid while on a scholarship. He served as the Head of the Fine Arts sector of Egypt's Ministry of Culture from 1988 to 2006. He was also Head of the Museums Sector of the Supreme Council of Antiquities in Egypt from 1994 to 1999, and founder of the Faculty of Fine Art, El Menia University, in Egypt. He currently heads the graphic department and teaches at the Faculty of Fine Arts at Cairo University.

Nawash graduated from the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome in 1964 and completed a degree in Graphics and Etching from the École des Beaux-Arts in Bordeaux in 1970 and also studied at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in Paris from 1975 to 1977.

Nseir studied painting at the Faculty of Arts in Cairo during the 1960s and returned to Syria to teach art at secondary schools in Latakia and Damascus. Her socialist, expressive work has influenced younger generations of Syrian artists.

Al-Obaidi earned his BFA from the Academy of Fine Arts in Baghdad in 1990 and went on to obtain an MFA in 1999 from the University of Guelph in Ontario, Canada, where he lived. He also studied Film Production and Direction in Los Angeles.

Osman spent five years in Rome where he completed a degree and MA in Sculpture from the Accademia di Belle Arti. Back in Egypt he served for 18 years as Head of the Sculpture Department at the Faculty of Fine Arts in Cairo, and then served as dean of the Faculty of Fine Arts in Alexandria, where he remained until his death in 1968. Osman is known for leading the Museum for Modern Art and the Faculty of Fine Arts in Cairo, as for devising a comprehensive plan to disassemble, relocate and assemble the Pharaohs of Abu Simbel.

Onsi graduated with a degree in Architecture from the Académie in Beirut in 1922, and apprenticed with the painter Khalil Saleeby at the time. For the next two years he was tutor to the prince of the Faisal family in Jordan.

After graduating from the Ente Nazionale Addestramento Lavoratori Commercio and Accademia San Giacomo in Rome in 1962, Lahham continued his studies in painting and etching at the Rochester Institute of Technology, New York. He is credited with designing many Jordanian currency notes, postage stamps, and medals. In 1971 Lahham was one of the founders of the Union of Arab Visual Artists. He has participated in most national group shows since 1951, and in 1991 he received the Jordanian State Appreciation prize for Visual Arts.

Laibi studied at the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad, 1967. Afterwards, he earned diplomas at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts and at the Sorbonne, both in Paris, completing the latter in 1981. Laibi is a member of several international and regional art groups including the French Artists Association and the Society of Iraqi Artists.

Madi trained in painting from 1958 to 1962 at the Académie Libanaise des Beaux-Arts in Beirut. In 1963 Madi went to study art at the Accademia di Belle Arti and the Accademia San Giacomo in Rome, where he lived for twenty-two years. He maintained ties with Lebanon by exhibiting work there and teaching at the National Institute of Fine Arts, until he finally relocated to Lebanon in 1986. Madi was appointed president of the Lebanese Association of Painters and Sculptors from 1982 to 1992.

Mahdaoui attained a degree from Accademia Sant'Andrea in Rome and another from the École du Louvre in Paris in 1967. He is a member of the International Jury of the Arts Prize of UNESCO, and participated in the second Havana Biennial in 1986.

Baya, born Fatma Haddad, was a self-taught artist. She lost her parents at a young age and was adopted by the French artist, Marguerite Caminat. Her first exhibition was held at Galerie Maeght, Paris in 1947, where her work caught the attention of Pablo Picasso and André Breton, who declared it Surrealist. Later in life she received second prize from Algeria's Union Nationale des Arts Plastiques in 1971, and also participated in the second Havana Biennial in 1986.

Mansour studied at the Bezalel Art Academy in Jerusalem. Though primarily known as a painter, he has worked as a cartoonist and writer. Mansour is the co-founder and Director of the Wasiti Art Center in Jerusalem, and is also a member of the New Vision group. Mansour is the co-author of Both Sides of Peace: Israeli and Palestinian Political Poster Art published in 1998. He received the Award at the 1998 Cairo Biennale.

Massoudy began training in Baghdad as an apprentice calligrapher under Hisham al-Baghdadi in 1961. He moved to study figurative art at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in Paris in 1969, but continued work with calligraphy, creating headings for Arabic magazines. Since then he has published several books and completed various studies on traditional and modern calligraphy. He regularly opens his studio to the public, and offers workshops to amateur and professional calligraphers.

El-Melehi studied at the School of Fine Arts in Tétouan, Morocco, in 1955. He then continued his education in Seville and Madrid, studied sculpture, ceramics in Rome and learnt engraving in 1964, he returned to Morocco to teach at the School of Fine Arts alongside Farid Belkahia and Mohammed Chebaa. El-Melehi was head of the Art Department at the Ministry of Culture in Morocco from 1985 to 1992. With Mohammed Benaïssa, he started a cultural festival in his hometown in 1978.

Mohammed joined the Free Art Atelier in 1960 where he studied painting at the Académie Julian. He later returned to Beirut, where in 1957 he founded the Lebanese Artists Association with George Corm and Rachid Wehbé, among others. Onsi received the Lebanese Order of Merit in 1955, and the National Order of the Cedar in 1958.

Owais studied in Cairo, graduating in 1944 from the School of Fine Arts and the Institute of Art Education in 1946. He became a champion of social realism and was a founding member of the Modern Art Group. Owais has exhibited worldwide and won major prizes throughout his career, including the 1956 Guggenheim International Prize and the Pioneer in Arts Prize at the 1997 Alexandria Biennale.

Son of miniaturist Ali Racim, Mohammed Racim studied at the Algerian École des Beaux-Arts before going on to work at the Manuscripts Department at the Bibliothèque Nationale in Paris. Before returning to Algeria, Racim was awarded a grant to travel and study Islamic Art in Cordoba and Granada. In 1933 he taught miniature painting at his alma mater in Algiers, and won the Grand Prix Artistique d'Algérie the same year.

After graduating from the School of Fine Arts in Cairo in 1948, Rafi earned his art diploma from the Higher Institute of Art Education in 1950. Later he won a scholarship to study for a Ph.D. in art history at the Sorbonne in Paris in 1953. Along with Abdel Hadi el-Gazzar and artist and scholar Hussain Youssef Amin, he was a founding member of the Contemporary Art Group in 1946. Rafi published widely in art journals, often conducting interviews with international artists such as Pablo Picasso and Salvador Dalí.

Al-Rahhal graduated from the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad in 1947 and went to study in Rome on a government scholarship in 1954. He received his MFA from the Accademia de Belle Arti in 1964. Al-Rahhal's work handling artifacts at Iraqi archaeological excavations complemented his development as a sculptor. He was commissioned to construct numerous public statues in Baghdad, and is most known for the Monument of the Unknown Soldier, 1982. Al-Rahhal was a vigorous proponent of the Baghdad Modern Art Group, and exhibited as a painter as well.

Al-Rassam studied Military Science and Art in Istanbul, and worked with several different artists during his time there. Al-Rassam was a member of the Society of the Friends of Art in Baghdad and is known to have created an iconic mural for the first Iraqi film theater, The Royal Cinema.

Al-Rawi graduated from the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad, 1959 and received a scholarship to study art in Yugoslavia in 1962. He was a curator at the Iraqi Museum of Art in Baghdad, and Chair of the Department of Fine Arts at the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad. Also an art historian and critic, he has written many essays and several books, and hosted Iraq's first and main television show about art for thirty years.

Largely self-taught, R'bati made his way through a lifetime of professions while maintaining a tremendous art output. A laborer in Marseille, a cook in Spain, and a bank employee, he eventually turned art-dealer in Tangiers as he used his experiences and travels as the main source of inspiration. Upon his return to Morocco, Rbati was warmly received due to the support and patronage of the Director-General of Fine Arts.

Sabbagh was educated by French Jesuit priests in Cairo before he left for Paris at the age of twenty-one. He was the first Egyptian to study at the Académie du Louvre, where he was taught by Maurice Denis, Paul Sérusier, and Félix Vallotton. He had his first exhibition in Paris in 1916. Sabbagh became a French citizen in 1930, but he maintained his ties with Egypt, traveling frequently to paint and teach art in Cairo. In 1933 the Egyptian government commissioned him to catalogue state-owned French treasures, and in Algeria in 1944 he curated an exhibition of French art gathered from museums in Cairo, Alexandria, Beirut, and Jerusalem.
In 1937 Sabri won a government scholarship to study at the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome.

Salim graduated with a law degree and then studied at the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad for three years. He left Baghdad for his work with the Foreign Service, taking up postings in many places, including Damascus and Khartoum. Salim continued to devote himself the arts with sporadic showings notably in Bonn and Stockholm. A major retrospective of his work was held in 1979. He was the brother of Jawad and Naziha Salim.

Salim studied painting and graduated in 1946 from the Academy of Fine Arts in Baghdad. She continued her studies with a higher diploma in fine arts at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in Paris, where she studied with Fernand Léger in 1951. Salim then returned to Baghdad. She taught at the Institute of Fine Arts and exhibited with the Baghdad Modern Art Group throughout the 1950s. She was the sister of Jawad Salim.

Samra graduated with honors from the École National Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in Paris in 1980. Then he moved to Saudia Arabia where he worked first as a stage designer then a graphic designer. In 1987 he returned to Paris to become a fine art and graphic consultant at the Institut du Monde Arabe. Samra has since lived and worked in Beirut and taught drawing and painting at the College of Fine Arts in Amman.

Salim was awarded government scholarships to study sculpture in Paris, Rome and London and was a founding member of the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad in 1949 and head of its Sculpture Department. In 1958 he was also a founding member of the Baghdad Modern Art Group with Shakir Hassan Al Said, and a founding member of the Society of Iraqi Plastic Arts. A landmark in Baghdad is Selim's Nasb al-Hurriyah (Monument of Freedom), created in commemoration of the 1958 revolution, became an important symbol around which a new Iraqi national identity was formed.

Salim graduated from the Slade School of Fine Art in London in 1948. There she met Jewad Selim, and they married in Baghdad in 1950. Selim taught drawing and painting at the Girls College and at the Architecture Department of the Engineering College, in Baghdad. During the 1950s she exhibited her paintings with the Pioneers Group, including Faiq Hassan and Ismail al-Shaikhly, as well as with the Baghdad Modern Art Group.

In 1945 al-Shaikhly completed his studies at the Institute of Fine Arts in Baghdad and continued his studies in Paris at the École Nationale Supérieure des Beaux-Arts in 1951. He was a founding member of the Pioneers Group with Faiq Hassan and also a founder of the Society of Iraqi Plastic Arts in 1956. He later became the Director General of the Directorate of Plastic Arts in Baghdad.

Shakir completed a degree in Ceramics from the Institute of Fine Arts, Baghdad in 1959. Following that he received a scholarship to study ceramics at the Central School of Art and Design in London in 1961, where he later worked and lectured. In 1966 Shakir moved back to Baghdad and became a ceramics instructor at the Institute of Fine Arts until 2001. He is known for his iconic ceramic murals mounted in various locations around Baghdad.

Sharif began his career as a caricaturist in the United Arab Emirates. He went to the United Kingdom and completed an art foundation course at Warwickshire College near Birmingham, and then received a degree in fine art and design at Byam Shaw School of Art in London in 1984. He returned to Dubai, where he founded the Emirates Fine Art Society the same year. He also founded the Group of Five with four other artists, and soon after he founded the Art Atelier in the Youth Theater in 1987.

Shawa first studied art at the Leonardo da Vinci Art Institute in Cairo from 1957 to 1958. In 1964 she pursued further studies at the Accademia di Belle Arti in Rome. Upon her return to Gaza, she taught art to children at UNRWA schools and was a UNESCO education lecturer. She moved to Beirut, where she took up illustrating children's books, continuing to contribute to Gaza by designing and installing stained-glass windows at the Rashad Shawa Cultural Centre established by her father. In 1987 she moved to London in order to devote herself solely to her art practice.

El-Shazly graduated in 1968 with a degree in Architecture from the Faculty of Fine Arts, Helwan University in Egypt. He has lived and worked in the United Arab Emirates for many years as an art instructor and architect. He has designed over thirty permanent school theaters, school landscapes, and the VIP area in the Abu Dhabi airport. He is also chief engineer at Egyptian Television in Cairo. Among the numerous recognitions el-Shazly has garnered for his work, in 1973 he received the Cairo Salon Prize, and in 1984 the Sheikh Khalifa Bin Zayed Prize.

Shukri was one of the first Iraqi to study art in England. He graduated with a degree in Arts in 1930 before returning to Iraq. In 1941 he became the president of the Art Friends Society in Baghdad and in 1947, Shukri took up a position as King Faisal's personal art instructor. One of the pioneers of Iraqi modern art, Shukri played an active role in the Union of Iraqi Artists in the late 1950s, and was its President from 1963 to 1964.

Gazbia Sirry received a BA in painting and a diploma in art education from the Higher Institute of Fine Arts for Girls in Cairo in 1948. She completed her postgraduate study at the Slade School of Fine Art at University College London in 1955. On her return to Cairo, Sirry became a member of several art groups, including the International Association of Art Critics, and the Egyptian Society for Graphic Artists. She received numerous international awards such as the Prix de Rome in 1952, and a fellowship at the Huntington Hartford Foundation Fellowship Award in California in 1965. She received Egypt's State Merit Award in 2000.

country of residence: Amman, Jordan and The Hague, the Netherlands
biography text: Talib studied at the College of Arts in Baghdad from 1961 to 1966. In 1980 he completed his MA in Graphic Design in Cairo. Talib taught at the Academy of Fine Arts in Baghdad, Iraq from 1980 to 1990 and at the Al-Yarmouk University in Jordan from 1991 to 1997. He was a founding member of the Innovationists and the Shadow Group in Baghdad. He won First Prize at the 1986 Baghdad International Festival of Plastic Art, and at the 1995 Sharjah Biennial.

Tamari graduated from the Beirut College for Women in Lebanon in 1966. She studied ceramics at the Istituto Statale per la Ceramica in Florence in 1972 and earned her M.Phil. in Islamic Art and Architecture from At Oxford University Tamari earned her Ph.D. in Islamic Art and Architecture in 1984. She has exhibited with the League of Palestinian Artists and is a founding member of the New Vision group and the Wasiti Art Center in Jerusalem. Tamari has published articles on Islamic ceramics, and is a coauthor of The Palestinian Village Home, 1989. She currently teaches Islamic Art and Architecture at Birzeit University.

Umar grew up in Baghdad and was one of the first women in Iraq to receive a government scholarship to study art in Europe. She graduated from the Maria Grey Training College in London in 1933. She taught painting at the Teachers Training School for Women, Baghdad, and was later became head of the Arts Department. In 1942 Umar moved to Washington, D.C., where she studied art education at George Washington University and earned an MFA from the Corcoran School of Art in 1959. Umar later returned to Baghdad and taught at the Academy of Fine Arts. She was a member of the One Dimension Group founded in 1971.

Wanly trained under the Italian artist Otorino Becchi and later taught painting in his own studio, and at the Faculty of Fine Arts in Alexandria. After his mentor's death in 1934, Wanly set up a private studio, and in 1942 set up another with his brother Seif that was accessible to any person interested in art and its applications.
